title: Uncial capital forms
subtitle: Writing the rounded single-case hand of early Christian manuscripts
excerpt: Uncial is a single-case script, it has no separate uppercase or lowercase, that was the dominant book hand of early medieval Europe from the 4th to the 8th century. Its round, open letterforms and modest pen angle make it a relatively accessible broad-edge script for calligraphers who have learnt the foundational hand.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906, John Hogg), chapter on insular and uncial scripts. Public domain. The analysis of the Book of Kells-influenced half-uncial hand is based on Johnston's traced exemplars of insular uncial letters.

glossaryTerms.definition: A broad-edge script written at a low nib angle (15-20°) with rounded, compact letterforms. Used for Christian manuscripts from the 4th to 8th centuries; revived by the Arts and Crafts movement in the late 19th century.
A later development of uncial in which some letters have longer ascenders and descenders. The Book of Kells and the Lindisfarne Gospels are written in insular half-uncial.

content.text: is its openness: the letters are round and wide, written at a low nib angle that keeps both horizontals and verticals moderately weighted. Unlike Gothic or Rustica, uncial has no strongly dominant thick strokes. The hand is suited to informal display pieces, quotation panels, and illuminated page headings.

Uncial is written at a 4-5 nib-width x-height, with a short ascender zone of 2 nib-widths and a descender zone of 2 nib-widths. In pure uncial, very few letters use the ascender zone (only b, d, h, l); in
the ascender zone is more prominent. Rule a guide sheet with all three zones marked. The nib angle is 15-20° from horizontal.

Begin with the O: a wide, nearly circular oval built from two arcs, left arc first. The O in uncial is much rounder than in gothic or italic, aim for a circle rather than an ellipse. Build from O: C, E, G (a C with a horizontal crossbar at midheight). Then D (a straight left downstroke with a wide round right arch) and Q (an O with a small right tail below the baseline).

The uncial arch springs from the middle of the stem (unlike italic, where it springs from the top third). Practice N: two downstrokes connected by a diagonal cross-stroke that starts high and ends low. M has wide outer legs and a small arch. The straight letters I, L, F, T use flat horizontal entry and exit strokes at low nib angle, producing evenly-weighted serifs.

Three letters look distinctively different from modern forms. A: written as a curved form with no apex, a round back on the left and a small curved crossbar, producing a shape closer to a rounded lower-case 'a'. T: the horizontal crossbar is below the cap-height line rather than at it. H: the arch connects to the left stem about midway down, and the right leg drops only to the baseline rather than being as tall as the left.
